points: A pure substance has a single type of particle with fixed composition; a mixture contains two or more substances in any proportion, separated by physical methods. Mixtures are homogeneous (uniform — e.g., salt water, alloys, air) or heterogeneous (non-uniform — e.g., soil, oil and water).

A solution is a homogeneous mixture where particle size < 1 nm; it is stable, cannot be filtered, and does not show Tyndall effect. Concentration (mass %) = (mass of solute / mass of solution) × 100. Solubility is the maximum solute dissolvable at a given temperature, and generally increases with temperature.
Alloys (e.g., brass = 70% Cu + 30% Zn) are solid solutions — they are mixtures because they have variable composition, retain constituent properties, and cannot be separated by physical methods.

summary.category: Suspension, Colloid, and the Tyndall Effect
points: A suspension (particle size > 1000 nm) is heterogeneous, unstable (particles settle), visible to naked eye, scatters light, and can be separated by filtration — examples: chalk in water, muddy water.

A colloid (particle size 1–1000 nm) appears homogeneous but is heterogeneous; it is stable, cannot be filtered (centrifugation is used), and shows the Tyndall effect — the scattering of a light beam by colloidal particles. Examples: milk (emulsion), fog (aerosol), smoke (aerosol), jelly (gel).

The Tyndall effect distinguishes colloids from true solutions: sunlight through a forest canopy, a beam entering a dusty room, or a torch beam through milk all make the light path visible due to scattering by particles of 1–1000 nm size.

summary.category: Physical & Chemical Changes and Pure Substances
points: Physical changes (melting, dissolving, cutting) produce no new substance — chemical composition is unchanged and the change is generally reversible. Chemical changes (burning, rusting, electrolysis) form new substances with new properties and are generally irreversible.

Elements (e.g., gold, oxygen) are pure substances that cannot be broken down by chemical reactions. Metals are lustrous, malleable, ductile, sonorous; non-metals are poor conductors and not malleable; metalloids (Si, Ge, B) are intermediate. Only Hg (metal) and Br (non-metal) are liquid at room temperature.

Compounds are pure substances of two or more elements in a fixed ratio, with properties entirely different from constituent elements (e.g., FeS from Fe + S loses magnetism and gives H₂S with acid, not H₂). Compounds differ from mixtures: fixed vs variable composition, new vs constituent properties, chemical vs physical separation.
